At the&nbsp; same time, my dad’s company has done&nbsp;&nbsp; well under my sister who took it over with&nbsp; my brother-in-law, so they’re going great. Trey Lockerbie (12:17): I brought up the American dream element&nbsp;&nbsp; because it’s refreshing actually to hear this&nbsp; story because it is so inspiring and it almost&nbsp;&nbsp; seems like it’s straight out of a movie. Some of&nbsp; these early beginnings in Legalzoom feel that way&nbsp;&nbsp; as well, like the story of actually partnering&nbsp; with Robert Shapiro, the famous attorney.&nbsp;&nbsp; Walk us through this story and&nbsp; how you ended up meeting Robert&nbsp;&nbsp; and what you ultimately learned&nbsp; from partnering with him. Brian Lee (12:42): Sure. When we started Legalzoom,&nbsp;&nbsp; the internet was still relatively new and people&nbsp; were scared of it. Not a lot of people remember&nbsp;&nbsp; this, you’re too young to remember this, Trey.&nbsp; When it was new, people were scared to put their&nbsp;&nbsp; information online. They were very scared to put&nbsp; their banking information online or their credit&nbsp;&nbsp; card information, they weren’t sure where it&nbsp; was going. We knew that we had to fix that&nbsp;&nbsp; and bring credibility to the site instantly, and&nbsp; that’s when we started reaching out to attorneys&nbsp;&nbsp; that we knew because we knew a famous attorney as&nbsp; part of Legalzoom would lend a lot of credibility.&nbsp;&nbsp; Robert Shapiro was at the top of our list&nbsp; because he was just coming off the OJ trial,&nbsp;&nbsp; everyone in America knew him and&nbsp; he was right here in Los Angeles. Brian Lee (13:27): I reached out to my network,&nbsp;&nbsp; which was pretty small at the time, but&nbsp; no one knew him personally. I honestly,&nbsp;&nbsp; I called 411, this is before Google. 411&nbsp; was calling it for information. I got&nbsp;&nbsp; Robert Shapiro, Attorney, Central City. I&nbsp; called the number, it was about 9:00 at night,&nbsp;&nbsp; because I had my voice message written out and I&nbsp; was going to pitch him on Legalzoom or leave him a&nbsp;&nbsp; message. He actually picked up the phone and said,&nbsp; “This is Robert Shapiro. How can I help you?” I&nbsp;&nbsp; freaked out for a second, I said, “Robert Shapiro,&nbsp; the attorney?” He said, “Yes, how can I help you?”&nbsp;&nbsp; I told him, “Well, my name is Brian Lee and I’ve&nbsp; got this business idea called Legalzoom,” and the&nbsp;&nbsp; first thing he said was I’m not interested. He was&nbsp; just about to hang up on me, I thought and I said,&nbsp;&nbsp; “Well wait, how do you know you’re not&nbsp; interested if you don’t hear me out?” Brian Lee (14:12): He basically said,&nbsp;&nbsp; “You got two minutes.” In those two minutes, I&nbsp; told him the whole story for Legalzoom, the whole&nbsp;&nbsp; vision behind it. At the end of it, he said, “It’s&nbsp; something really interesting.” He said, “You know?&nbsp;&nbsp; I’ve actually been thinking of something similar,&nbsp;&nbsp; and so why don’t you call at a normal time&nbsp; tomorrow and set up an appointment to come&nbsp;&nbsp; in and we’ll talk?” That’s how we got Robert&nbsp; Shapiro involved and that’s how it started. Trey Lockerbie (14:34): Now, was Robert the only factor that built&nbsp;&nbsp; trust with customers? Did it just take a certain&nbsp; amount of time for people to get comfortable with&nbsp;&nbsp; the internet in general? Did you have to tread&nbsp; water for a little while that was developing? Brian Lee (14:48): It was interesting. I think Robert Shapiro&nbsp;&nbsp; brought a lot of credibility, but besides just the&nbsp; credibility piece, every time he would go onto CNN&nbsp;&nbsp; or this or that and had an interview, he would&nbsp; mention Legalzoom and we would get more orders.&nbsp;&nbsp; I think Robert played a huge role in bringing&nbsp; that credibility at the earliest stages.&nbsp;&nbsp; But then, what happens about in terms of building&nbsp; a brand, it does take time. It takes time.&nbsp;&nbsp; To build a brand, it takes time, it takes&nbsp; capital, but it also takes extreme consistency&nbsp;&nbsp; in your promise. I always tell a lot of&nbsp; entrepreneurs that it takes time to build a brand,&nbsp;&nbsp; but you’ll build brand if you just consistently&nbsp; deliver what you promise, and if you do that, then&nbsp;&nbsp; you’ll grow. Legalzoom was the same way. We got&nbsp; a little lucky with Legalzoom in terms of timing. Brian Lee (15:36): Not a lot of people know this,&nbsp;&nbsp; but we were one of the earliest companies&nbsp; who advertise pay for click. PPC advertising,&nbsp;&nbsp; pay per click advertising was super nascent in&nbsp; the earliest days. I had a friend who was at&nbsp;&nbsp; Idealabs in Pasadena then, they were working&nbsp; on a first paid search engine called goto.&nbsp;&nbsp; He called me and said, “Hey Brian, why don’t you&nbsp; try this out? You put money in this account and&nbsp;&nbsp; people do a search and you’ll get ranking based&nbsp; on how much you’re paying for a click.” I said,&nbsp;&nbsp; “Okay, well try it.” We stuck $20 into this&nbsp; account and after spending about a dollar&nbsp;&nbsp; or something, we got an order, like “Hey, this&nbsp; actually works.” We really grew with paid search.&nbsp;&nbsp; Initially, we were paying for … I’ll give you&nbsp; an example. The term incorporate back in the day&nbsp;&nbsp; when we first started, it was $0.1 per click.&nbsp; By the time I had left Legalzoom, people were&nbsp;&nbsp; paying upwards of $80 per click. It got a little&nbsp; bit insane, but we really ramped with paid search. Trey Lockerbie (16:34): What was the hero product,&nbsp;&nbsp; if you will, for Legalzoom early on? You were&nbsp; paying for clicks, but what were you trying&nbsp;&nbsp; to attract the customer to do and sell? What&nbsp; did the product roadmap look like early on? Brian Lee (16:47): Yeah. We really thought that we could&nbsp;&nbsp; really democratize law in a lot of ways.&nbsp; Incorporation services, trademark services,&nbsp;&nbsp; last will and testaments and living trusts&nbsp; were the core of what we did. These were what&nbsp;&nbsp; we considered very simple legal processes or&nbsp; documents that attorneys in general were still&nbsp;&nbsp; charging a lot for. We felt that the masses&nbsp; would prefer to pay, call it $99 to incorporate&nbsp;&nbsp; as opposed to $2,000 at that time. That was&nbsp; our thesis and sure enough, it was true.&nbsp;&nbsp; Legalzoom has incorporated more companies than&nbsp; any other entity in America at this point,&nbsp;&nbsp; and formed more wills and living trust than any&nbsp; other entity in America. We really ramped with the&nbsp;&nbsp; core legal document services. But then, we always&nbsp; had in the back of our heads that if we could&nbsp;&nbsp; dominate legal documents and become a brand name&nbsp; in law, we could extend into a myriad of other&nbsp;&nbsp; categories. That’s what was the vision behind&nbsp; Legalzoom, was building that brand name along. Trey Lockerbie (17:55): One thing you’re really well-known for is being&nbsp;&nbsp; this e-commerce expert, because you were so early&nbsp; on there and you almost pioneered a lot of these&nbsp;&nbsp; strategies. You mentioned the pay-per-click&nbsp; opportunity you had early on. Nowadays, and&nbsp;&nbsp; I don’t know if it was like this back then, but&nbsp; a lot of people refer to the metrics of customer&nbsp;&nbsp; acquisition cost or CAC basically, what it sounds&nbsp; like, the cost to acquire the customer, and LTV,&nbsp;&nbsp; lifetime value. You’re oftentimes referencing the&nbsp; two and how they go together. Lifetime value being&nbsp;&nbsp; the prospect of amount of dollars or revenue&nbsp; that’ll come from that single customer&nbsp;&nbsp; over time. These seem like relatively&nbsp; new metrics within the last decade,&nbsp;&nbsp; but that might just be my own personal experience&nbsp; talking. Given your background, you must have been&nbsp;&nbsp; pioneering these metrics early on. How aware of&nbsp; these strategies were you in those early days? Brian Lee (18:43): We were pretty aware&nbsp;&nbsp; of our metrics and our KPIs at Legalzoom.&nbsp; We would track them basically every minute.&nbsp;&nbsp; We knew exactly how much we were paying per&nbsp; click and how much revenue that was generating.&nbsp;&nbsp; Because we didn’t raise outside capital, we knew&nbsp; we had to be profitable within that first order.&nbsp;&nbsp; We knew how much money we could pay per&nbsp; click for a certain group of customers&nbsp;&nbsp; and that the payback would have to be&nbsp; immediate. Now, things did change over&nbsp;&nbsp; time because then you start realizing that&nbsp; that customer is more than that one order&nbsp;&nbsp; and that over the lifetime of that&nbsp; customer, they might come back and&nbsp;&nbsp; form another company or make a revision to their&nbsp; last will and testament, whatever that might be,&nbsp;&nbsp; but that came much later. What we were looking&nbsp; for initially was this is the price per click,&nbsp;&nbsp; this is how much we could afford, so that we&nbsp; profit along the payback on that first order. Brian Lee (19:34): Now the CAC to LTV&nbsp;&nbsp; ratios that you mentioned, we started implementing&nbsp; at ShoeDazzle years ago, more than a decade ago.&nbsp;&nbsp; We were one of the earliest to really do a lot of&nbsp; social media marketing and influencer marketing&nbsp;&nbsp; at ShoeDazzle. Just as Legalzoom grew with&nbsp; pay-per-click, ShoeDazzle grew with Facebook&nbsp;&nbsp; marketing. The earliest days of Facebook&nbsp; marketing, we fell into and we understood&nbsp;&nbsp; that because we were a subscription business for&nbsp; women’s shoes that we could pay ahead in terms&nbsp;&nbsp; of a certain CAC and over a two-year lifetime&nbsp; value, we could get paid back. That’s how you&nbsp;&nbsp; could get very, very aggressive with your spend.&nbsp; But you got to be careful with that, of course,&nbsp;&nbsp; because LTVs change. LTVs change and anyone who’s&nbsp; going out 3, 4, 5 years on a lifetime value,&nbsp;&nbsp; the world can change and a lot of other&nbsp; things could change and the costs on the&nbsp;&nbsp; acquisition side could start getting higher&nbsp; and higher and higher, so a lot can change.&nbsp;&nbsp; I actually always recommend, if possible, do a&nbsp; one or a two-year at max lifetime value model. Trey Lockerbie (20:44): Were there periods of growth that required&nbsp;&nbsp; deficit spending to get to where you needed&nbsp; to be? Or were you always growing responsibly? Brian Lee (20:51): No. We always grew responsibly, but we never&nbsp;&nbsp; really hit a deficit at Legalzoom. Legalzoom,&nbsp; it was never a sexy business, Trey. It was never&nbsp;&nbsp; like, “Hey, we’re going from zero to a hundred&nbsp; million overnight,” it was never that business.&nbsp;&nbsp; It was always, we grew Legalzoom call it 10% to&nbsp; 25% year-over-year profitably. When you look back&nbsp;&nbsp; 20 years later, it becomes a sizeable business.&nbsp; For us, we always just knew that we had to&nbsp;&nbsp; remain profitable. Now, we didn’t have enough&nbsp; profits to really try too many new things,&nbsp;&nbsp; but one big lesson I learned at Legalzoom, and&nbsp; I don’t share this often, I remember we got&nbsp;&nbsp; this one customer who ordered, I think&nbsp; it was nine divorces within a month&nbsp;&nbsp; and we were like, “Wow, this guy’s getting married&nbsp; and divorced a lot,” like nine times in a month. Brian Lee (21:42): I called him and I remember he was out in&nbsp;&nbsp; Florida. I called him up and I said, “Well, I’m&nbsp; just confirming your ninth divorce order here,”&nbsp;&nbsp; and he is like, “Yeah, absolutely,” and he&nbsp; says, “When can I get this?” I’m like, “Oh,&nbsp;&nbsp; we’ll have it to you by tomorrow.&nbsp; We’ll have all the documents done.”&nbsp;&nbsp; I realized that he was an attorney. He was a&nbsp; divorce lawyer using us to do all the backend&nbsp;&nbsp; documents for him. Of course, I “ding, ding,&nbsp; ding”, “Oh my goodness, we’re more than just a&nbsp;&nbsp; D2C company, we’re actually a B2B company&nbsp; too. We started thinking, “Wow, we could&nbsp;&nbsp; double our growth. We could grow into this other&nbsp; category.” This is about year two of Legalzoom,&nbsp;&nbsp; year three, maybe. We started&nbsp; building something called Proxy Law,&nbsp;&nbsp; which was a much more robust backend paralegal&nbsp; document service for attorneys, for law firms. Brian Lee (22:32): We were like, “Okay,&nbsp;&nbsp; we’re going to build this,” so&nbsp; we put all of our best resources,&nbsp;&nbsp; our best technologists, Eddie Hartman was&nbsp; working, we were all working on Proxy Law&nbsp;&nbsp; because we thought that was our next big venture.&nbsp; What we realized though was after we built it,&nbsp;&nbsp; this is a very different market. Knocking on doors&nbsp; of attorneys, getting them to try a SAS product&nbsp;&nbsp; was very different than marketing a last will&nbsp; or corporation services to an actual consumer.&nbsp;&nbsp; We almost went broke. There is one time&nbsp; where we couldn’t pay payroll because we sunk&nbsp;&nbsp; all of our money into Proxy Law. It’s just&nbsp; a lesson that I’ve always taken with me for&nbsp;&nbsp; every company. You just got to&nbsp; stay very focused in your core,&nbsp;&nbsp; make sure that you’re incredibly strong at your&nbsp; foundation before extending. Don’t extend until&nbsp;&nbsp; you’re ready. It’s just stuff that I took with&nbsp; me after we lost, went bankrupt at Legalzoom. Trey Lockerbie (23:24):&nbsp; It sounds like it was almost a blessing&nbsp; in disguise that you weren’t able to raise&nbsp;&nbsp; venture capital early on. Do you think that&nbsp; would’ve actually impacted the business given&nbsp;&nbsp; that it seemed to take a long time to build&nbsp; trust and for the internet to evolve? Do you&nbsp;&nbsp; think you could’ve even put that money&nbsp; to work the way you would’ve wanted to? Brian Lee (23:39): I think it was actually a blessing,&nbsp;&nbsp; Trey. I think I see it the same way&nbsp; as you do in that sense. I actually&nbsp;&nbsp; think if we had raised a boatload of money for&nbsp; Legalzoom, I don’t think it would’ve worked.&nbsp;&nbsp; I think just by being savvy and frugal, if you&nbsp; will, making sure that the growth is that we’re&nbsp;&nbsp; not outgrowing our own infrastructure and just&nbsp; being smart with capital, I think it really helped&nbsp;&nbsp; with Legalzoom. I think, because we’ll put it&nbsp; this way, there were probably five or six other&nbsp;&nbsp; legal companies that did receive venture funding&nbsp; back then and none of them are around today. Trey Lockerbie (24:11):&nbsp; You spent years and years on this thing and it&nbsp; became ultra successful. What was the day you&nbsp;&nbsp; woke up and you were saying to yourself, “All&nbsp; right, I think I’m good here. Time to move on.” Brian Lee (24:23): Yeah. It’s always tough&nbsp;&nbsp; to make that final decision to move on.&nbsp; But in my case, it really comes down to&nbsp;&nbsp; the best and happiest days of my life at&nbsp; Legalzoom, my career was at the earliest stages.&nbsp;&nbsp; You mentioned that I was eating ramen and peanut&nbsp; butter and jelly sandwiches, which is true,&nbsp;&nbsp; but I was happiest then. Just being&nbsp; in a room of four or five people,&nbsp;&nbsp; iterating and building very quickly and coming up&nbsp; with this ideas and trying things and so forth,&nbsp;&nbsp; that is fun for me. I’m addicted to startups&nbsp; in that sense. Now, once a company scales to a&nbsp;&nbsp; thousand employees, it’s just a very different&nbsp; company than it was in their earliest stage.&nbsp;&nbsp; I just find a lot more enjoyment at the early&nbsp; stages of the business as opposed to the later&nbsp;&nbsp; stages, where there’s just a lot more involved in&nbsp; terms of structure, organization, decision-making,&nbsp;&nbsp; a lot of outside factors, your&nbsp; investors and this and that.&nbsp;&nbsp; There’s a lot more going on in terms of that&nbsp; as opposed to just building. I like building. Brian Lee (25:25): I’ll give another example&nbsp;&nbsp; at one of my more recent companies. I remember I&nbsp; wanted to change the color of one of the buttons,&nbsp;&nbsp; the continue buttons to green, as opposed to the&nbsp; color yellow that it wasn’t. I remember I was&nbsp;&nbsp; talking to the product team, I said, “Why don’t&nbsp; we try making that green?” They were like, “Okay,&nbsp;&nbsp; we’ll do that.” Then a couple of weeks passed&nbsp; and it’s still yellow and then finally,&nbsp;&nbsp; we have this meeting and there’s probably&nbsp; 15 people in the room and they said, “Well,&nbsp;&nbsp; we’ve done all these tests and the right color&nbsp; is purple.” I was thinking to myself, “Well,&nbsp;&nbsp; that’s great.” Really, it’s terrific that we&nbsp; decided, that he did the research and did all&nbsp;&nbsp; the testing and everything else and purple’s the&nbsp; right answer. Terrific. Let’s go with purple. Brian Lee (26:07): In my head, I’m thinking, “I wanted a green.” Does&nbsp;&nbsp; that make sense? Little things like that, that’s&nbsp; when you start realize, “You know what? It’s not&nbsp;&nbsp; moving as quickly as I wanted to, and I really&nbsp; should go off the data,” and there’s people who&nbsp;&nbsp; are really good at it, Trey. Take some famous&nbsp; folks that probably were on your show before.&nbsp;&nbsp; I don’t know, Jeff Bezos or Elon Musk or&nbsp; whoever these ultra-successful folks are,&nbsp;&nbsp; they could scale from zero to billions and&nbsp; not miss a beat. Honestly, I’m not like that.&nbsp;&nbsp; I’ll be honest with it. I’m really good&nbsp; at the earliest stages. I’m probably not&nbsp;&nbsp; the guy you want running a multi-billion&nbsp; dollar business. I’ll be honest with you. Trey Lockerbie (26:49): I was interested about that because the people&nbsp;&nbsp; you just mentioned there, they’re very bombastic,&nbsp; they have really interesting reputations.&nbsp;&nbsp; Bezos, even Bill Gates, they’re often quoted&nbsp; or references telling employees that was the&nbsp;&nbsp; stupidest idea I’ve ever heard. They’d lash&nbsp; out because they’re so intelligent and they&nbsp;&nbsp; don’t hide it. You are not a very boastful person,&nbsp; and from just being with you a short while,&nbsp;&nbsp; I can tell you have this very polite manner&nbsp; about you. It makes me curious about your style&nbsp;&nbsp; as a CEO, because I know there’s lots of different&nbsp; flavors out there, there’s lots of ways to do it.&nbsp;&nbsp; What was your style building&nbsp; culture in those companies early on? Brian Lee (27:25): I think culture is extremely important&nbsp;&nbsp; at every company. I think once you&nbsp; establish a culture that’s working,&nbsp;&nbsp; stick with it, because if you change it, things&nbsp; fall apart. We all know there’s a very different&nbsp;&nbsp; culture at Disney as opposed to Zappos or Nike&nbsp; or name the company. They have their own cultures&nbsp;&nbsp; and it works for them. In my case, the culture&nbsp; that I like to set is one of understanding,&nbsp;&nbsp; communication. I like to lead by,&nbsp; basically, I have a lot of empathy&nbsp;&nbsp; towards people and the team, and I really love&nbsp; people working together and iterating together.&nbsp;&nbsp; It’s more just honest, open communication,&nbsp;&nbsp; and this worked. This worked for me. I would say,&nbsp; some of the hardest things I’ve ever had to do&nbsp;&nbsp; were make very firm decisions on firing&nbsp; people. It’s always been hard for me,&nbsp;&nbsp; Trey. I know some CEOs that have no&nbsp; problem, “Hey, get rid of these folks.” Brian Lee (28:26): I’ve always had hesitancy when it came to firing&nbsp;&nbsp; people or laying people off or this or that. I&nbsp; remember we had my second ShoeDazzle. One of the&nbsp;&nbsp; hardest things I had to do was lay off 50% of the&nbsp; people in one day, and it was miserable for me.&nbsp;&nbsp; I care a lot about our team. I actually believe&nbsp; that you’ll hear a lot from entrepreneurs who say,&nbsp;&nbsp; “Oh, well the customer is always write and the&nbsp; customer comes first, do right by the customer.”&nbsp;&nbsp; I’ve always actually said, “The team comes first.”&nbsp;&nbsp; The team comes first because if you&nbsp; have a well-oiled team, a driven team,&nbsp;&nbsp; a team that’s working well together, that’s&nbsp; everything, because that leads to happy customers.&nbsp;&nbsp; It leads to a better product,&nbsp; better service, better customers. Trey Lockerbie (29:12): We’re to come back to team, but&nbsp;&nbsp; since you mentioned ShoeDazzle there, I have heard&nbsp; you say that was a low point and it was somewhat&nbsp;&nbsp; of a short-lived part of your career, it would&nbsp; seem. What were the dynamics playing out there? Brian Lee (29:23): Yeah. ShoeDazzle, it was a really fun business&nbsp;&nbsp; to start. Started it with Kim Kardashian and she&nbsp; was terrific. She’s one of the hardest working&nbsp;&nbsp; women I think I’ve ever met, Trey. She’s all over&nbsp; it. We grew it, we launched it, it grew very, very&nbsp;&nbsp; quickly. Then, about three years into it, that’s&nbsp; when I was going to join The Honest Company with&nbsp;&nbsp; Jessica Alma to start The Honest Company, so we&nbsp; found a CEO to run ShoeDazzle and it just didn’t&nbsp;&nbsp; quite work. Part of it was a culture shift, part&nbsp; of it were for some other reasons, the model was&nbsp;&nbsp; not working as well as we wanted it to.&nbsp; I came back in to help with ShoeDazzle&nbsp;&nbsp; and it was tough. That’s when we laid off half&nbsp; the people. We were rejiggering a lot of things. Brian Lee (30:10): Luckily, we sold it to a competitor and&nbsp;&nbsp; our investors actually were made whole, which&nbsp; was fantastic, but it was just hard. It was just&nbsp;&nbsp; hard. It was just anytime you have to go in and&nbsp; change a lot of major things, it is never easy.&nbsp;&nbsp; There are a lot of days at ShoeDazzle where we&nbsp; couldn’t pay our bills. We had lawyers chasing us,&nbsp;&nbsp; we had the electric company chasing us, we had&nbsp; everyone chasing us. It was a scary time and I&nbsp;&nbsp; know a lot of entrepreneurs have been there,&nbsp; but luckily we landed the plane and got out. Trey Lockerbie (30:40): From partnering with&nbsp;&nbsp; Robert Shapiro early on, you learned the&nbsp; power of media and you partnered with Kim&nbsp;&nbsp; on ShoeDazzle. I’m curious to know how that came&nbsp; about. Were there other considerations? Was Kim&nbsp;&nbsp; always at the top of your mind as far as the&nbsp; right person for this product in particular? Brian Lee (30:57): Yes, we knew Kim because&nbsp;&nbsp; her father used to work with Robert Shapiro, my&nbsp; partner at Legalzoom. We would see Kim at some of&nbsp;&nbsp; his events or at dinner or this or that. It was&nbsp; my wife that really wanted to get Kim involved&nbsp;&nbsp; because she had watched the very first&nbsp; episode of Keeping up with the Kardashians&nbsp;&nbsp; and my wife, Mira, said, “She’s going to&nbsp; be huge. We should go talk to Kim about&nbsp;&nbsp; ShoeDazzle.” That’s who we talked to. We really&nbsp; didn’t talk to anyone else. It was really Kim&nbsp;&nbsp; that we were focused on and sure enough,&nbsp; it worked and we couldn’t afford her today,&nbsp;&nbsp; but back then she was just&nbsp; getting started and so it worked. Trey Lockerbie (31:36): That was great foresight by your wife. Brian Lee (31:38): Yeah. Trey Lockerbie (31:38): I’d like to keep moving&nbsp;&nbsp; here onto The Honest Company, which is also&nbsp; now public. I have never really believed in&nbsp;&nbsp; overnight success stories until I&nbsp; studied up on The Honest Company,&nbsp;&nbsp; because while it might seem like it took some&nbsp; time from the concept to the actual launch,&nbsp;&nbsp; this business was doing something around&nbsp; $160 million or so in revenue in year two,&nbsp;&nbsp; which is just extraordinary. What do you think&nbsp; ultimately contributed to this initial success? Brian Lee (32:08): A lot of it has to do with Jessica Alba,&nbsp;&nbsp; who was so incredibly passionate about the&nbsp; product and what we were building. Honest Company,&nbsp;&nbsp; more so than any other company I’ve started, was&nbsp; extremely mission-driven, and that the vision&nbsp;&nbsp; of trying to create a non-toxic world really&nbsp; resonated with a lot of folks from consumers who,&nbsp;&nbsp; especially our own team members to everyone&nbsp; involved in it, we were very driven by that&nbsp;&nbsp; mission. With Jessica, she was so incredibly&nbsp; passionate, she would do every interview,&nbsp;&nbsp; she would promote it and it really&nbsp; resonated with a lot of moms.&nbsp;&nbsp; We knew that it would because at the time&nbsp; we started The Honest Company together,&nbsp;&nbsp; it was really a time where eco was really&nbsp; picking up a lot of steam. Eco was going into&nbsp;&nbsp; the mainstream, but no one was actually talking&nbsp; about chemicals as opposed to the environment. Brian Lee (33:04): I, inherently, knew that&nbsp;&nbsp; a mother would pay even a slight premium&nbsp; for the safety and well-being of her child.&nbsp;&nbsp; They might not pay that slight premium to save&nbsp; the environment necessarily. Everyone wants to&nbsp;&nbsp; do good for the environment. We all do, but do&nbsp; we want to pay for it? We will pay for health&nbsp;&nbsp; and safety for sure. That’s why I knew&nbsp; that little nuance for The Honest Company,&nbsp;&nbsp; that lane to go into non-toxic, chemical free&nbsp; was a pretty large lane that was wide open. Trey Lockerbie (33:42): It’s hard to even imagine having&nbsp;&nbsp; enough infrastructure in place to execute that&nbsp; level of revenue so quickly. What were a couple&nbsp;&nbsp; of the major growing pains you remember most&nbsp; from that year one to year two to year three? Brian Lee (33:54): Oh yeah. There was a lot of growing pains, a lot&nbsp;&nbsp; of sleepless nights. Yeah, it was really, again,&nbsp; bringing in the right people at the right time.&nbsp;&nbsp; We couldn’t have done it without the team that&nbsp; we put in place. We were having a lot of issues.&nbsp;&nbsp; When you grow that quickly, you have a lot&nbsp; of eyeballs on you watching your every move,&nbsp;&nbsp; and so we had to be ultra-careful in terms of&nbsp; everything that we said and did and produced and&nbsp;&nbsp; everything else. There are so many things that we&nbsp; learned early on that we had to fix on the flight.&nbsp;&nbsp; It was almost like flying a plane and fixing&nbsp; the engines in the air. It’s the same old&nbsp;&nbsp; story. Basically, I remember we created these&nbsp; laundry pods once and they were great products,&nbsp;&nbsp; it was great pods and we would ship them and we&nbsp; never really tested them in freezing cold weather. Brian Lee (34:41): When we shipped them back&nbsp;&nbsp; east in the winter, they would explode on people’s&nbsp; front porches. Stories like that were abundant,&nbsp;&nbsp; that we had to fix as we were going along. It&nbsp; was just a lot of hustle. It was a lot of hustle.&nbsp;&nbsp; A lot of great partners that we worked with that&nbsp; would iterate on products very quickly for us and&nbsp;&nbsp; work with us. We had a company called Valor that&nbsp; was making our diapers down in Mexico and they&nbsp;&nbsp; were fantastic partners. They would fix any issues&nbsp; that we would have and so forth. It was really&nbsp;&nbsp; just getting to that type of volume that quickly&nbsp; too, working with factories that could crank out&nbsp;&nbsp; that type of product and hit our growth rates that&nbsp; we’re hitting and working with us. It wasn’t easy&nbsp;&nbsp; by any means, Trey. It was just more working in&nbsp; concert with team, partners, vendors, all of it. Trey Lockerbie (35:33): Having a celebrity like Jessica Alba&nbsp;&nbsp; as a partner is obviously very helpful. There’s a&nbsp; lot of exposure that comes with that, especially&nbsp;&nbsp; early on, but having a celebrity isn’t everything.&nbsp; A lot of celebrity-driven products have failed,&nbsp;&nbsp; a lot. In your opinion, what is missing in&nbsp; some of these brands that don’t make it? Brian Lee (35:53): I actually think it’s not&nbsp;&nbsp; necessarily the celebrity, it’s mostly the team.&nbsp; It’s mostly the team. If you’re a celebrity,&nbsp;&nbsp; you have to surround yourself with the&nbsp; right team that can execute on your vision.&nbsp;&nbsp; Also, it has to be authentic. I’ve seen a lot of&nbsp; times where a celebrity will promote something&nbsp;&nbsp; or an influencer will promote something&nbsp; that just doesn’t make sense to promote.&nbsp;&nbsp; You’ll have an MMA wrestler who wants to start&nbsp; a furniture company. What do you have to do with&nbsp;&nbsp; furniture? I don’t know. Sometimes there’s that&nbsp; disconnect, but if there really is authentic&nbsp;&nbsp; belief in what you’re doing and people find&nbsp; it, consumers find it to be authentic then,&nbsp;&nbsp; and if it doesn’t work, it’s probably the&nbsp; team. I actually always, as an investor&nbsp;&nbsp; myself, I invest in teams. I don’t invest in&nbsp; ideas. Ideas are a dime a dozen, as you know. Trey Lockerbie (36:44):&nbsp; With The Honest Company, you set&nbsp; out and raised quite a bit of money.&nbsp;&nbsp; Unlike Legalzoom, you raised $27 million,&nbsp; I believe, at the launch. How did you end&nbsp;&nbsp; up at that number? I’m also curious how you set&nbsp; evaluation given that the company was pre-revenue? Brian Lee (36:58):&nbsp; I knew we had to raise a&nbsp; significant amount of money,&nbsp;&nbsp; because I knew the roadmap, the volume of product&nbsp; that we had to bring in the door for these types&nbsp;&nbsp; of products from the baby shampoos, the wipes,&nbsp; to the diapers was significant. You can’t just&nbsp;&nbsp; order 10 packs of wipes to get started. You’re&nbsp; going to have to order millions of dollars worth.&nbsp;&nbsp; I also knew that the advertising was getting&nbsp; expensive. Unlike Legalzoom where it was cheap,&nbsp;&nbsp; it was one cent per click or unlike ShoeDazzle&nbsp; where we latched onto the earliest years of&nbsp;&nbsp; Facebook marketing where it was inefficient&nbsp; and so we took advantage of that inefficiency.&nbsp;&nbsp; That wasn’t the case for The Honest Company.&nbsp; We had to raise enough capital to go out there&nbsp;&nbsp; and really promote it and market it and advertise&nbsp; and so forth, so we needed some more capital. Brian Lee (37:46): Fortunately, we had the right&nbsp;&nbsp; partners who gave us that capital, Light Speed,&nbsp; General Catalyst and IVP. Those three believed&nbsp;&nbsp; in it. IVP was an investor in Legalzoom,&nbsp; Light Speed was an investor in ShoeDazzle&nbsp;&nbsp; and General Catalyst were friends of&nbsp; mine. I’m still close with Neil Sequeira,&nbsp;&nbsp; who’s now at Defy. They just believed&nbsp; in us. The craziest thing happens, Trey,&nbsp;&nbsp; is when you’re an entrepreneur first starting&nbsp; out, it’s tough, honestly, to raise capital&nbsp;&nbsp; and we all know that, and you really have to&nbsp; hustle and round up as much money as you can.&nbsp;&nbsp; When you have a company that’s already&nbsp; successful, it is so much easier to raise capital.&nbsp;&nbsp; Success begets success. Raising that $27&nbsp; million was actually not that difficult.&nbsp;&nbsp; We had even more than the $27 million available&nbsp; to us. We could have probably raised $50m or $60m&nbsp;&nbsp; to start the company, but we needed about $27m,&nbsp; $28m to hit our plans for the next two years. Trey Lockerbie (38:40):&nbsp; A lot of entrepreneurs romanticize about the&nbsp; day of going public because it represents a&nbsp;&nbsp; finish line to some degree, even though&nbsp; it’s a starting line in a lot of ways,&nbsp;&nbsp; but for a lot of founders, it’s the opportunity to&nbsp; cash in on the sweat equity they put in early on.&nbsp;&nbsp; Not that was necessarily or your route, I’m&nbsp; not sure, but I’m curious because The Honest&nbsp;&nbsp; Company and Legalzoom are both now public.&nbsp; They both went public about a year ago.&nbsp;&nbsp; Was there anything meaningful to you about&nbsp; the companies actually going public? Were they&nbsp;&nbsp; too far in the rear view at that point to&nbsp; really care or feel anything from that? Brian Lee (39:13): Well, no. Absolutely felt a lot of pride&nbsp;&nbsp; in having these companies go public and&nbsp; being part of them, part of their history.&nbsp;&nbsp; But to me, it was just, I don’t&nbsp; know, it’s more just a check mark.&nbsp;&nbsp; I’m taking a company public, started&nbsp; a company that went public. It wasn’t&nbsp;&nbsp; a cause for celebration. That’s the&nbsp; new structure of the company, you have&nbsp;&nbsp; public investors and that’s that. It’s just&nbsp; another period, a season of the company. Trey Lockerbie (39:42): See, I find that so funny because&nbsp;&nbsp; it’s often that you romanticize about these things&nbsp; and when they finally come, you’re just like, “All&nbsp;&nbsp; right, another day, let’s keep going.” Was the&nbsp; idea of going public for either of those companies&nbsp;&nbsp; something that got you out of bed in those early&nbsp; days with that dream or something of that sort,&nbsp;&nbsp; was that something kind of driving you early on? Brian Lee (39:59): No. I never was driven by taking a company public.&nbsp;&nbsp; I’m driven by building something great.&nbsp; I like building things that will last&nbsp;&nbsp; and be around for decades. That’s what&nbsp; drives me. It is the impact that I like to&nbsp;&nbsp; make as opposed to dreaming of taking a company&nbsp; public. It’s more, can we build a beautiful&nbsp;&nbsp; business that has staying power? That’s much more&nbsp; fulfilling to me than the IPOs or even selling a&nbsp;&nbsp; company for a large amount. It’s really, is this&nbsp; company going to be around? Will my grandchildren&nbsp;&nbsp; say, “My granddad started that company or helped&nbsp; start that company.” That’s what I look for. Trey Lockerbie (40:40): I think that’s exactly why you are as&nbsp;&nbsp; successful as you are. You’ve had this opportunity&nbsp; now to sit on the other side of the table as&nbsp;&nbsp; a VC and one of your early checks was into&nbsp; a company called Honey that’s now gone on&nbsp;&nbsp; to sell to PayPal for $4 billion, and PayPal’s&nbsp; obviously another public company now. What did&nbsp;&nbsp; you see in this company, Honey, early&nbsp; on that led to such a massive success? Brian Lee (41:01): I saw a strong team.&nbsp;&nbsp; I saw George and Ryan. George Ruan and&nbsp; Ryan. I remember I gave us a talk once at&nbsp;&nbsp; an accelerator program. I remember they came up to&nbsp; me afterwards and they were describing what they&nbsp;&nbsp; were working on and it sounded interesting.&nbsp; I took a meeting with them afterwards and&nbsp;&nbsp; I just really liked them. I thought they were&nbsp; incredibly smart. They seemed very dedicated&nbsp;&nbsp; to what they were going to build, because this is&nbsp; the earliest stages of Honey is where we invested.&nbsp;&nbsp; I honestly just like them. It’s hard to describe,&nbsp; but I just have this sense of entrepreneurs and&nbsp;&nbsp; if they’re the type of&nbsp; entrepreneurs I want to back,&nbsp;&nbsp; meaning will they just keep going? Are they&nbsp; going to give it their a hundred percent all&nbsp;&nbsp; the time? If this doesn’t work, basically,&nbsp; what happened? You want entrepreneurs like&nbsp;&nbsp; this has to work for them and I’ve got that&nbsp; feeling from them, from George and Ryan. Trey Lockerbie (42:00): Well, you’ve now had an&nbsp;&nbsp; opportunity to become a CEO again, which is&nbsp; also interesting. This time you’re partnering&nbsp;&nbsp; with Derek Jeter. Talk to us about Arena Club and&nbsp; why you felt compelled to start building again. Brian Lee (42:13): I’m so happy you asked about Arena Club. Yeah.&nbsp;&nbsp; I’m super excited to be CEO again for the fourth&nbsp; time. We’re launching the company on September 8,&nbsp;&nbsp; knock on wood, hopefully. Derek Jeter’s been&nbsp; fantastic to work with on this. It’s in the&nbsp;&nbsp; trading card category, and so baseball, basketball&nbsp; cards. We were using computer vision and machine&nbsp;&nbsp; learning to grade sports cards and then we&nbsp; digitize them, put them on blockchain and then&nbsp;&nbsp; create these digital marketplaces where you can&nbsp; buy, sell and trade cards with other showrooms,&nbsp;&nbsp; other online showrooms. That’s really the idea.&nbsp; I’ve been collecting cards my whole life. I&nbsp;&nbsp; started as a kid and I never stopped collecting&nbsp; cards in high school, in college, law school.&nbsp;&nbsp; I remember when I was Skadden, my&nbsp; very first paycheck from the law firm,&nbsp;&nbsp; I bought more cards. It’s something&nbsp; that I’ve been doing for a long time. Brian Lee (43:03): I started collecting with my son&nbsp;&nbsp; probably about six years ago&nbsp; when he was about seven years old&nbsp;&nbsp; and my happiest days are really going to&nbsp; the card shops and card shows with him.&nbsp;&nbsp; I took this hobby/passion into starting a company&nbsp; here. I’m building something that I would want as&nbsp;&nbsp; a hobbyist, as a collector, and it’s just&nbsp; something that’s never really existed where&nbsp;&nbsp; you have this social marketplace as opposed&nbsp; to what exists today, it is much more about&nbsp;&nbsp; community and a club, which is why it’s called&nbsp; arenaclub.com. We’re super excited to launch&nbsp;&nbsp; it on September 8 and hopefully some of&nbsp; your listeners will come check it out. Trey Lockerbie (43:42): Absolutely.&nbsp;&nbsp; Derek Jeter is obviously a&nbsp; legend, but there’s a lot of&nbsp;&nbsp; amazing baseball players out there. Why Derek&nbsp; Jeter? How did that relationship come to be? Brian Lee (43:51): Well, he’s a captain.&nbsp;&nbsp; That’s why. I actually considered Derek Jeter one&nbsp; of the greatest ball players who have ever lived,&nbsp;&nbsp; who have ever swung a bat. Just his leadership&nbsp; ability, his demeanor, he’s incredibly smart&nbsp;&nbsp; and dedicated, and so I think, and this really&nbsp; resonates with him, and so through some mutual&nbsp;&nbsp; friends, I got to know Derek and I think&nbsp; it’s going to be a fantastic launch for&nbsp;&nbsp; us and we’ll see what happens. You&nbsp; never know, so we’ll see what happens. Trey Lockerbie (44:21): Now, are you finding that&nbsp;&nbsp; you are bringing a certain playbook to each&nbsp; company that maybe iterates over the life&nbsp;&nbsp; of each company or is it starting fresh and&nbsp; dependent on the opportunity and the team? This upbringing provided a structured&nbsp;&nbsp; environment, but it also presented a unique set&nbsp; of social barriers as she navigated the outside&nbsp;&nbsp; world. Her father managed parking lots, while&nbsp; her mother, Norma Jean, spent over forty years&nbsp;&nbsp; as a kindergarten teacher. Her family played a pivotal role in shaping her&nbsp;&nbsp; character, even when their paths diverged. Personal Life.&nbsp; While her professional life is often under the&nbsp; bright lights of the paparazzi and award shows,&nbsp;&nbsp; Quinta Brunson has worked incredibly hard to&nbsp; maintain a personal life that is defined by&nbsp;&nbsp; privacy, groundedness, and deep familial loyalty.&nbsp; In September two thousand twenty-one, she quietly&nbsp;&nbsp; wed her longtime partner, Kevin Jay Anik, in&nbsp; a celebration that was remarkably understated&nbsp;&nbsp; for a woman of her fame. Kevin, who operates&nbsp; primarily outside the entertainment industry,&nbsp;&nbsp; has been described by those close to the couple&nbsp; as Quinta’s ultimate support system. Their&nbsp;&nbsp; relationship is a sanctuary of normalcy; she&nbsp; often credits him with providing the stability&nbsp;&nbsp; she needs to navigate the pressures of being a&nbsp; global icon. While they occasionally grace the&nbsp;&nbsp; red carpet together for major events, they&nbsp; rarely share the intimate details of their&nbsp;&nbsp; marriage on social media, choosing instead to&nbsp; keep their most precious moments for themselves.&nbsp; Family has always been the North Star for&nbsp; Quinta. As the youngest of five children,&nbsp;&nbsp; her identity is deeply rooted in the bustling,&nbsp; faith-filled household she grew up in back in&nbsp;&nbsp; West Philadelphia. Even as her net worth has&nbsp; soared and she has acquired luxury estates,&nbsp;&nbsp; she remains intensely connected to her roots.&nbsp; She frequently returns to Philadelphia to spend&nbsp;&nbsp; time with her siblings and her mother, Norma&nbsp; Jean, whose career in education provided the&nbsp;&nbsp; soul for "Abbott Elementary." Early in the run of her hit show,&nbsp;&nbsp; Quinta faced minor controversy regarding the&nbsp; nuanced portrayal of the American education&nbsp;&nbsp; system, specifically the debate between public and&nbsp; charter schools. - How Does Taylor Swift Handle Taxes? - The Rich And Famous Files: Kind: captions Language: en How does Taylor Swift handle taxes? Have you ever wondered how a superstar like Taylor Swift manages her taxes? With her incredible success and wealth, tax management is a big part of her financial strategy. Let's break it down in a way that makes sense. As one of the top musicians in the world, Taylor Swift faces unique tax challenges. When she performs in different states or countries, she incurs tax liabilities in each location. This is often referred to as jock taxes, a term used for athletes and entertainers who pay taxes in places where they earn income. This means that in addition to her home state taxes, she has to file taxes in every state where she performs. Navigating the world of taxes is not simple. Taylor has to deal with federal, state, and local tax requirements. Each of these levels has its own rules, and they can get complicated quickly. When her tours extend beyond the United States, she also has to comply with international tax laws. These laws can vary greatly from one country to another, adding another layer of complexity to her tax situation. To manage her tax burden, Taylor can take advantage of various tax credits and deductions. For example, charitable contributions can provide significant tax writeoffs. This means that when she donates to causes she cares about, she can reduce the amount of income that is taxable. Property taxes are another area where she might find some relief. If she makes renovations or owns specific types of properties, she could benefit from property tax reductions. Additionally, strategic donations can yield tax credits. In Pennsylvania, for instance, donations can provide credits of up to 90% of the donation value. When it comes to her tours, the revenue generated is massive, but a significant portion goes toward expenses, including taxes. For example, her United States tour is expected to net her around $550 to million after expenses, but before taxes. This shows how important it is for her to manage her finances carefully. Taylor likely employs sophisticated financial planning and works with tax advisers to navigate the complex tax landscape. This helps her maximize profitability and minimize tax liabilities. By doing this, she can reinvest more of her earnings into her music and other business ventures, which helps her wealth grow even further. For rich and famous celebrities like Taylor Swift, managing taxes effectively is essential. It ensures that their financial success is not overly diminished by tax obligations. Taylor's approach reflects a balance between meeting her financial responsibilities and strategic planning to support her career and business ventures.

- How Do Celebrities Transfer Wealth To Their Spouses?: Kind: captions Language: en Imagine having an incredible fortune and wanting to ensure your partner is financially secure, no matter what the future holds. For celebrities, with their immense wealth, securing a spouse's financial well-being is a crucial part of their financial planning. Celebrities typically transfer wealth to their spouses through a combination of strategic estate planning tools and legal provisions, most notably utilizing the unlimited marital deduction under United States tax law. This allows them to move assets between spouses without incurring immediate gift or estate taxes, providing flexibility and security for their loved ones. The core mechanism enabling these substantial transfers is indeed the unlimited marital deduction. This provision states that any amount of property given or bequeath to a spouse who is a United States citizen is exempt from federal gift tax or estate tax. This key advantage ensures that wealth can pass freely between spouses, postponing any potential estate tax liability until the surviving spouse's passing, thereby providing time for further financial planning and management. For practical application, celebrities employ several methods. Direct outright gifts are common, simply transferring ownership of assets like property or investments. More sophisticated strategies involve various types of trusts such as marital trusts or qualified terminable interest property trusts often called QTIP trusts. These trusts allow the celebrity to provide for their spouse's needs while maintaining some control over the ultimate distribution of assets to other beneficiaries like children after the spouse has passed. Joint ownership of assets such as bank accounts or real estate with rights of survivorship is another straightforward way to ensure assets passed directly to the surviving spouse outside of probate. What many do not realize is that these strategies are not solely about avoiding taxes at death. They are also powerful tools for asset protection, shielding wealth from potential future creditors or legal challenges, and can even facilitate income splitting for tax planning during the couple's lifetime. The careful selection and structuring of these tools often involve a team of specialized estate attorneys and financial adviserss, tailoring plans to the unique circumstances and immense asset portfolios of each celebrity family. Ultimately, the goal is to create a robust and flexible framework that safeguards the spouse's financial future, minimizes tax burdens over generations, and respects the celebrities wishes regarding their legacy by leveraging established legal provisions and sophisticated planning. They ensure their partners are well provided for, allowing their substantial wealth to continue supporting their families for years to come.

- Everyone Wants a Beautiful Home But Few Can Afford: Kind: captions Language: en Humans are social creatures. Since birth, humans are not alone but live in harmony with their families, societies, and surrounding environments. Just as humans need food, clothing, and education to survive in society, they also need a safe and beautiful place to live. A house is not just a place to rest. It is a symbol of human security, comfort, love, and family ties. Therefore, every human being has a dream in their mind to build their own beautiful home. A beautiful home brings peace of mind to people's lives. When someone returns to their home after a tiring day, the atmosphere, decor, and comfort of that home soothes their mind. A neat and tidy home is not only visually appealing, it also makes people's mental state positive. A beautiful and comfortable home also plays a significant role in strengthening love and relationships among family members. But the reality is that building a beautiful home is not that easy. It requires a lot of money, planning, and time. Nowadays, due to the increase in land prices, the increase in construction materials, and the increase in labor costs, building a home has become difficult for the common man. This dream is often impossible to realize especially for the middle and lowerass people. They struggle to meet their needs at the end of the day. And raising a large sum of money to build a house is a big challenge. Many people work hard throughout their lives just to fulfill their dream of a small house. Some save for years while others try to build a house by taking a loan from the bank. But due to the burden of debt, installment pressure and uncertain income, many cannot fulfill that dream. As a result, they are forced to live in a rented house, which further increases their mental and financial stress. However, despite limited capabilities, it is possible to build a beautiful house by adopting some strategies. For example, using small space properly, reducing unnecessary expenses, building a house step by step, etc. In modern times, new methods and technologies have come to build houses at a much lower cost, which shows some hope for the common people. In addition, it is possible to build a neat and beautiful house at a low cost by adopting a minimalist lifestyle. The beauty of a house does not depend only on its size or expensive furniture. Rather, its beauty depends on its tidy environment, proper management and the love of the family members. Even a very small house can be very beautiful and comfortable if it is decorated properly and the people living there are happy. There is also a difference between houses in the village and in the city. Although the cost of building a house in the city is high, the facilities are more. On the other hand, in the village, it is possible to build a house in a large area at a low cost, but the modern facilities may be somewhat less. Therefore, people decide according to their needs and capabilities where and how to build a house. Finally, it can be said that a beautiful house is an important dream of a person's life. However, to fulfill this dream, relying only on money will not work. Proper planning, patience and awareness are required because a house is not just a brick and stone structure. - Who Are the Current Celebrities Living in LA’s Most Exclusive Neighborhoods? - Everything About LA: Kind: captions Language: en Who are the current celebrities living in law's most exclusive neighborhoods? Have you ever wondered which celebrities call Los Angeles home? The city is famous for its exclusive neighborhoods that attract stars seeking luxury and privacy. Let's take a closer look at some of these areas and the notable residents who live there. Belair is one of the most sought-after neighborhoods in Los Angeles. It is known for its sprawling mansions and gated estates. Beyonce and Jay-Z own an impressive compound valued at $88 million. Their home features modern design and smart home technology along with extensive grounds. Other famous residents in Belair include Jennifer Lopez and Elon Musk, both of whom have luxurious estates in this prestigious area. The combination of old Hollywood charm and modern opulence makes Bair a favorite among celebrities. Next up is Beverly Hills, a classic celebrity hot spot. This neighborhood is famous for its upscale shopping and iconic landmarks like Rodeo Drive and the Beverly Hills Hotel. Taylor Swift owns a historic Georgian style manor on Laurel Lane, previously owned by film director Samuel Goldwin. Other stars living in Beverly Hills include Jennifer Lawrence, who resides in the gated Hidden Valley Estates, and Denzel Washington, who calls the ultra-private Beverly Park community home. This area also houses Justin and Haley Bieber, showcasing the appeal of Beverly Hills to high-profile couples. The Hollywood Hills attract those who appreciate stunning views and unique architecture. Jennifer Aniston owns a mid-century modern estate designed by architect A. Quinsey Jones. She renovated the property to blend old world and new world design elements with beautiful Japanese inspired gardens. Other celebrities who have lived or currently resided in the Hollywood Hills include Reese Witherspoon, Harry Styles, and Robert Patterson. This area offers a mix of natural beauty and proximity to studios, making it a popular choice for actors and musicians. In Los Angeles, the most exclusive neighborhoods are home to a diverse mix of music icons, actors, entrepreneurs, and industry leaders. These areas provide privacy, luxury, and convenience with properties ranging from historic estates to cuttingedge modern homes. Belair, Beverly Hills, and the Hollywood Hills stand out as primary residences for celebrities like Beyonce, Jay-Z, Jennifer Lopez, Elon Musk, Taylor Swift, Jennifer Lawrence, Denel Washington, and Jennifer Aniston, reflecting the varied tastes and lifestyles of Los Angeles elite.

- 3 Tips For First-Time Home Buyers | Money | TIME: Kind: captions Language: en [Music] the biggest issue I see with first-time home buyers is they get into the process too early because it's so easy to find homes online to find listings there's so many websites now it's so easy just to pick up the phone and call a broker they go out and start shopping based on what they think they want when at the end of the day what you need the Home you're going to buy has nothing to do with what you think you want on day one my three tips are first-time home buyers is one get a pre-approved that's it it is super easy call up a banker walk into a bank get preapproved for a loan if you are getting a loan and you're running around looking for a home and you are not pre-approved already it's like going to the mall without a wallet it's a waste of your time because maybe you can afford a lot more and then why are you seeing all these crappy houses maybe you can afford a lot less and you need to bring yourself down just a little bit tip number two I a little biased but I would say always work with a broker a buyer's broker in the United States is paid by the seller the seller is going to pay a commission either just to their agent or they're going to pay it to their agent and to your agent and the third tip I would give firsttime buyers is to really buy within your means the economy is in a funky place the global market is in a funky Place do not stretch yourself if you really really feel uncomfortable with it because you never know what can happen

- 15 Celebrities Who Hid Their Secret Relationship For A While: Kind: captions Language: en There aren't many things you can hide as a celebrity and public figure, especially when everyone wants to know what's going on in your personal life. But these celebs have figured out how to keep their relationships very private and only reveal what they want when they want. For some, that's with red carpet debuts and candid interviews. For others, it required walking down the aisle. Here are the celebs that kept their relationship ultra low-key. Number one, Zendaya and Tom Holland. Tom and Zendaya romance rumors have been swirling since they first starred in Spider-Man: Homecoming together. Are you Spider-Man's girlfriend? But they denied the allegations of a romantic relationship for years. Like when they joked about a headline claiming they were in love and vacationing together all summer in 2017. Finally, in the summer of 2021, they were caught sharing a kiss in Tom's Audi. And from then on, they've been giving couple goals with their support for each other. Tom was even the first person Zendaya texted after she won her second Emmy. So cute. Number two, Rihanna and Travis Scott. This relationship was a blink and you'll miss it situation. Yes, way back before they were parents, Rihanna and Travis dated in absolute secrecy. During Travis Scott's December 2015-January 2016 Complex cover interview, former Complex editor Lawrence Schlossman wrote that Travis dodged questions about her but described her as a muse and kept him waiting so Travis could hang out with Rihanna. Since both Travis and Rihanna are notoriously private about their personal lives, it's no surprise this relationship was so under the radar. Number three, Taylor Swift and Joe Alwyn. The only way we learn about Taylor Swift's relationship is through her music. And even then, it's like putting together a puzzle. When Taylor and Joe first got together, Taylor was sneaking to London to spend time with Joe in his North London home. She would slip away across the pond undetected. It wasn't until a year into their relationship in October 2017 when Taylor revealed she was in a relationship at a listening party for her Reputation album. Number four, Chris Evans and Alba Baptista. Chris Evans has been talking about wanting to settle down for some time. So it's not surprising he's in a relationship with Portuguese actress Alba Baptista. Although the couple haven't come out to say they're officially off the market, a source told people they've been dating seriously for over a year. Apparently, we skipped a lot of chapters because Emily shared on Busy Philipps's talk show, Busy Tonight, that she had vetted Sebastian for two years as a friend but that she always knew there would be something more. Though they've gotten divorced, they share a son together. Number six, Jennifer Lawrence and Cooke Maroney. Even though they're married, Jennifer Lawrence and art gallerist Cooke Maroney are very private with their relationship. You rarely see them on a red carpet together and only catch them together dressed down, out and about, just doing normal things. Number eight, Emma Stone and Dave McCary. Emma's key to keeping her relationship with SNL writer and director Dave McCary private may be due to her lack of social media presence. Even her engagement announcement didn't come from her. It was shared on her husband's page. They had quietly dated for a few months before news broke that they were seeing each other. And even then, neither of them officially confirmed anything until the ring shot. They quietly welcomed a daughter they are also privately raising. Number nine, Sydney Sweeney and Jonathan Davino. Even though they were spotted attending events together in 2018, Sydney still played it coy when she told Elite Daily she would settle down in Chicago, saying she had a friend that lived there. Even with engagement rumors out there because of the ring she was wearing on her ring finger, Sydney still won't talk about her man. She's keeping things really private. Number 10, Suki Waterhouse and Robert Pattinson. This Batman likes to keep things a mystery when it comes to his love life. Suki has also kept things under wraps but let it be known she is not just somebody's nobody girlfriend, calling out the writers of the Gossip Girl reboot for the joke about her and the Twilight star. Number 11, Leighton Meester and Adam Brody. This couple is so private they didn't attend a red carpet together for over two years. Then they got engaged just a few months later in November 2013 and got married in February 2014. And they made their grand debut as a couple on the red carpet for the Tony Awards.
